messages.content:
Hey — full policy set is in place from our five prior sessions. Snyk gate, QA integration test authorship, the audit pass, and the SECURITY.md/COMPLIANCE.md additions to required_artifacts that finally landed in the last session under v0.3.0.

What are we updating today?

Hi, since we last spoke, you've had a number of upgrades. So, given your new updates and the updated model that is under your hood, I need you to please review the existing .agentpolicy/ files, primarily the constitution, governance, and roles files. Look for correctness, ensuring they adhere to the project's goals and parameters, as well as adhere to the v0.3.0 aegis-spec that you are now operating from. If there are any discrepencies that you want to note in the governance docs, bring them to my attention. If everything appears correct, then I will need a proper handoff prompt to give an agent, either Claude Code or Codex, to implement the updates into the codebase. In our past attempts, the handoff prompt you gave me created problems with the agent, that then surfaced several underlying gaps in the aegis stack, hence the upgrades you've gotten that I mentioned in the beginning. Let me know if you have questions, otherwise, please proceed with your audit.

Got it. Let me do a careful pass through the three files and report back. I have governance and the role files in full from the briefing, and I have constitution complete through the principles, sensitivity tiers, and build commands. That should be enough — but if I find a gap that needs the tail of a file I don't have inline, I'll pull it.

Let me work through this systematically.

**constitution.json**

Self-identifies as v0.3.0 ✓. Project identity, module map, and domains are internally consistent — nine modules, nine domain entries, paths match. Tech stack is duplicated at `project.stack` and top-level `tech_stack`, which is the schema's intentional shape (one nested under project, one as a sibling) — not a bug.

Ten principles, cleanly numbered 1–10, each with matching `name`/`id` pairs and `enforcement: "hard"`. The ten immutable_policies in governance.json all resolve to these principle IDs — I confirmed this in the prior audit session and it still holds.

`required_artifacts` now has all three entries: README.md, SECURITY.md, COMPLIANCE.md. That's the v0.3.0 landing we expected.

Sensitivity tiers (restricted/high/medium/low) and build_commands are present and well-formed.

One thing I want to flag, not as a defect but as a small consistency note: the README in the repo describes a three-tier model (Restricted / High / Standard) while the constitution defines a four-tier model (restricted / high / medium / low). Both are internally consistent, but the README's "Standard" tier maps to two tiers in the constitution. Not a governance problem — the constitution is the source of truth for agents — but worth knowing if anyone reads both documents and gets confused.

**governance.json**

Self-identifies as v0.3.0 ✓. Autonomy levels match what we set: conservative on compliance and customers, advisory everywhere else. The `autonomy.domains` and `autonomy.domain_overrides` duplication I flagged in the prior audit is still there — still benign, still slightly redundant, but nothing breaks because of it.

Permissions, sensitive_patterns, escalation, override_protocol, conventions, cross_domain_rules, data_directory_policy — all consistent with the constitution and with the role files.

Quality gates section is comprehensive: tests, lint, typecheck, unit-tests, integration-tests (scoped to regulated domains), audit-trail-verification (scoped to financial/PII domains), qa-signoff, and snyk-security-scan. The Snyk gate is present both as a `custom_check` under `pre_commit` and as a top-level entry in the `gates` array — that's the schema's intentional shape (custom_checks define the command, gates define the policy), so it's correct.

The seven conventions — including `integration-tests-by-qa-only` — are all present with correct scopes and enforcement levels.

**Role files (all seven)**

All self-identify as v0.3.0 ✓. Each role has the expected scope (primary_paths, secondary_paths, excluded_paths), autonomy override matching governance, and the `integration_test_authorship: "qa-only"` convention plus the corresponding forbidden action.

QA role has the expanded write access to `*/tests/` directories with the `write_mode_exceptions` carve-out preserving append-only on `qa/reports/`. Integration test authorship block is present and scopes correctly to all seven testable domains.

Compliance and customers roles correctly carry `autonomy: "conservative"` matching governance. All escalation triggers are coherent.

**One genuine discrepancy worth your attention**

The `default` role's `paths.read` is set to `["shared/"]` only. Looking at the QA role's design philosophy — read access to all domains, write only to its lane — and at the catch-all nature the default role is supposed to serve, this feels overly narrow. If an agent ends up assigned the default role for any reason (an agent without a specialist match, or a discovery/audit task), it can't read anything outside `shared/`. That makes the default role nearly useless for its stated purpose.

Two reasonable fixes: either (a) widen `default.paths.read` to include all domains read-only with `paths.write` staying empty, matching the audit-style permissions QA has, or (b) leave it as-is and accept that the default role is intentionally minimal — only invoked for trivial shared-folder work — and any meaningful task requires explicit role assignment.

I lean toward option (a) — it makes the default role usable as a "read-only auditor with no write privileges anywhere," which is a useful safety default. But this is your call. It's not a regression, it's an original design choice from session 1 that may or may not be what you want now.

**Everything else**

Ledger is empty with the correct write protocol shape. Overrides log has the two historical entries we discussed. No silent drift, no orphaned IDs, no semantic regressions. The accumulated changes from the five prior sessions are all present and consistent.

So here's my recommendation: your governance is in good shape. The one real call to make is whether to widen `default.paths.read`. Everything else is either intentional or already correctly landed.

Want me to update the default role, or leave it alone? Once you decide, I can produce the handoff prompt.

Let's leave the default role as-is for now. If we ever encounter an edge case where that role is blocking a necessary task, we can address it then. But for now, that role is almost unneeded, since there are sufficient specialist roles as well as a catch-all construction role via the aegis-mcp. the default role is a spec standard, but we effectively have adapted the spec to this project so well, that the default role is pretty much not needed. So, if everything else seems to be in order, the main thing I need from you is a very specific handoff prompt for an agent, that addresses the fact that this is an existing and complete codebase, which just needs targeted updates with the recent changes that haven't been committed or implemented, particularly the 3rd party testing elements.

Makes sense — leaving default as-is is the right call given the role landscape you've built.

Before I draft, let me confirm what I'm putting in the handoff so we're aligned on scope.

The receiving agent (Claude Code or Codex) is operating on an existing, complete codebase — 109 tests across 23 suites, all seven domains stood up, governance fully in place. This is not a build, not a restructure, not a scaffold. The agent's job is targeted implementation of the accumulated uncommitted policy changes.

Specifically, the agent needs to do four things. First, call `aegis_policy_summary` and select the QA role — QA is the right operating role because the work spans cross-domain verification, integration test authorship for the new third-party validation pattern, and creation of audit-evidence artifacts. Second, review the uncommitted policy diff to understand what's now in force: the Snyk security scan gate in governance, the `integration-tests-by-qa-only` convention propagated across governance and all seven role files, the quality gate additions (snyk-security-scan, audit-trail-verification, qa-signoff, integration-tests scoping), and the three required_artifacts entries (README.md exists, SECURITY.md and COMPLIANCE.md need creation). Third, implement the third-party testing infrastructure — wire Snyk into the GitHub Actions CI workflow as a required gate alongside typecheck/lint/test, ensure the existing test suite cleanly separates unit tests (domain-authored, fine as-is) from integration tests (QA-authored going forward, no rewrite of existing tests required, just enforcement of the convention from this point on). Fourth, create SECURITY.md (security policy and vulnerability disclosure, content derived from the constitution's sensitivity tiers and principles plus the README) and COMPLIANCE.md (PCI-DSS, SOX, AML/KYC, and data privacy posture, content derived from the README and constitution's compliance sections), then update .gitignore to exclude `.agentpolicy/sessions/` and `.agentpolicy/state/overrides.jsonl`, and commit everything together.

Explicit guardrails in the prompt: do not scaffold, do not restructure, do not rewrite existing domain code, do not author new integration tests for existing code (the convention applies going forward), do not modify governance policy files, escalate before any cross-domain change.

One question before I draft: on the Snyk wiring — do you have a Snyk account and `SNYK_TOKEN` already configured as a GitHub Actions secret, or should the prompt instruct the agent to set up the workflow with a placeholder and flag the secret-provisioning step back to you? That changes whether the agent can complete the CI integration end-to-end or needs to hand off to you for the final step.
Excellent catch! I do not have that setup yet as a GitHub Actions secret, so the agent will need to loop me in when it's time to take that step

Perfect — I'll have the prompt instruct the agent to wire the Snyk workflow with the standard `${{ secrets.SNYK_TOKEN }}` reference and then explicitly pause and surface the secret-provisioning step to you before attempting any CI run that depends on it. The workflow file lands committed and ready; the secret is the gate you control.

So here's the full scope I'll bake into the handoff:

The agent calls `aegis_policy_summary`, selects the QA role, reviews the uncommitted policy diff, then executes four targeted tasks. One: wire Snyk into `.github/workflows/ci.yml` as a required gate alongside typecheck/lint/test, using `${{ secrets.SNYK_TOKEN }}`, then pause and notify you to provision the secret in GitHub before merge. Two: verify the existing test suite respects the `integration-tests-by-qa-only` convention going forward — no rewriting existing tests, just enforcement of the convention from this point. Three: create SECURITY.md (derived from constitution sensitivity tiers, principles, and the README) and COMPLIANCE.md (derived from README and constitution compliance sections). Four: update `.gitignore` to exclude `.agentpolicy/sessions/` and `.agentpolicy/state/overrides.jsonl`, then commit policy changes and new artifacts together.

Explicit guardrails: do not scaffold, restructure, or rewrite existing domain code. Do not author new integration tests for existing code — the convention applies going forward only. Do not modify governance policy files. Escalate before any cross-domain change. Pause and loop the human in before any step that depends on the SNYK_TOKEN secret.

Sound right?
